coredata.dc:title: High harmonic generation in graphene quantum dots with vacancy defects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gnawali, Suresh
Apalkov, Vadym
coredata.dc:description:
We study theoretically the generation of high-order harmonics in hexagonal and triangular graphene quantum dots (QDs) with vacancy defects in the field of an optical pulse. For intrinsic QDs, some high-order harmonics are suppressed due to symmetry of the systems. Then, the main effect of vacancies is the breaking of the QD’s symmetry and the enhancement of the corresponding harmonics. For a hexagonal QD, for an intrinsic case, even-order harmonics are suppressed for a linearly and circularly polarized pulses, while
3 m
-order ( m is an integer) harmonics are also suppressed for a circularly polarized pulse, with a monovacancy, all high-order harmonics are strongly enhanced. Suppression and enhancement of high-order harmonics for triangular QDs with armchair edges is similar to the case of hexagonal QDs. For triangular QDs with zigzag edges, all high-order harmonics are visible in the intrinsic case, while the presence of a monovacancy enhances the intensities of all harmonics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
High harmonic generation
Nonlinear optics

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: US Department of Energy Office of Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Major funding was provided by Grant No. DE-FG02-01ER15213 from the Chemical Sciences, Biosciences and Geosciences Division, Office of Basic Energy Sciences, Office of Science, US Department of Energy. Numerical simulations were performed using support by Grant No. DE-SC0007043 from the Materials Sciences and Engineering Division of the Office of the Basic Energy Sciences, Office of Science, US Department of Energy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Energy spectra of hexagonal GQDs with zigzag edges: intrinsic GQDs (no vacancies) (a), (d), and (g); GQDs with monovacancy (b), (e), and (h); GQDs with divacancies (c), (f), and (i). The number of atoms in each GQD is shown in each panel. The insets in each panel illustrate the GQD systems.

Energy spectra of triangular GQDs with zigzag edges: intrinsic GQDs (no vacancies) (a), (d), and (g); GQDs with monovacancy (b), (e), and (h); GQDs with divacancies (c), (f), and (i). The number of atoms in each GQD is shown in each panel. The insets in each panel illustrate the GQD systems.

Energy spectra of triangular GQDs with armchair edges: intrinsic GQDs (no vacancies) (a) and (d); GQDs with monovacancy (b) and (e); GQDs with divacancies (c) and (f). The number of atoms in each GQD is shown in each panel. The insets in each panel illustrate the GQD systems.

Emission spectra generated by hexagonal GQDs placed in the field of an optical pulse. The size of GQD is
N = 24
[(a) and (b) panels],
N = 54
[(c) and (d) panels], and
N = 84
[(e) and (f) panels]. The number of vacancies,
N
v s c
, which is 0, 1, or 2, is marked in each panel. The amplitude of the incident pulse is 0.3 V/Å. The pulse is linearly polarized [panels (a), (c), and (e)] and circularly polarized [panels (b), (d), and (f)].

Emission spectra generated by triangular GQDs with zigzag edges in the field of an optical pulse. The size of GQD is
N = 22
[(a) and (b) panels],
N = 33
[(c) and (d) panels], and
N = 61
[(e) and (f) panels]. The number of vacancies,
N
v s c
, which is 0, 1, or 2, is marked in each panel. The amplitude of the incident pulse is 0.3 V/Å. The pulse is linearly polarized [panels (a), (c), and (e)] and circularly polarized [panels (b), (d), and (f)].

Emission spectra generated by triangular GQDs with armchair edges in the field of an optical pulse. The size of GQD is
N = 36
[(a) and (b) panels] and
N = 60
[(c) and (d) panels]. The number of vacancies,
N
v s c
, which is 0, 1, or 2, is marked in each panel. The amplitude of the incident pulse is 0.3 V/Å. The pulse is linearly polarized [panels (a) and (c)] and circularly polarized [panels (b) and (d)].

Emission spectra of triangular GQD with zigzag edges and number of atoms
N = 22
[panel (a)] and hexagonal GQD of size
N = 24
[panel (b)]. The incident optical pulse is linearly polarized. The pulse’s amplitude is marked in each panel. The number of vacancies,
N
v s c
, which is 0, 1, or 2, is also shown in each graph.

Emission spectra of triangular GQD with zigzag edges and number of atoms
N = 22
[panel (a)] and hexagonal GQD of size
N = 24
[panel (b)]. The incident optical pulse is circularly polarized. The pulse’s amplitude is marked in each panel. The number of vacancies,
N
v s c
, which is 0, 1, or 2, is also shown in each graph.
Bandgaps for different types of quantum dots with different number of vacancies.

head.ce:title: High harmonic generation in graphene quantum dots with vacancy defects

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Astronomy, Georgia State University, Atlanta, GA 30303, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Astronomy, Georgia State University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Astronomy, Georgia State University, Atlanta, Georgia 30303, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
We study theoretically the generation of high-order harmonics in hexagonal and triangular graphene quantum dots (QDs) with vacancy defects in the field of an optical pulse. For intrinsic QDs, some high-order harmonics are suppressed due to symmetry of the systems. Then, the main effect of vacancies is the breaking of the QD’s symmetry and the enhancement of the corresponding harmonics. For a hexagonal QD, for an intrinsic case, even-order harmonics are suppressed for a linearly and circularly polarized pulses, while
3 m
-order ( m is an integer) harmonics are also suppressed for a circularly polarized pulse, with a monovacancy, all high-order harmonics are strongly enhanced. Suppression and enhancement of high-order harmonics for triangular QDs with armchair edges is similar to the case of hexagonal QDs. For triangular QDs with zigzag edges, all high-order harmonics are visible in the intrinsic case, while the presence of a monovacancy enhances the intensities of all harmonics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Exploring 2D materials, such as graphene, transition metal dichalcogenides, and their derivatives, has recently opened up a captivating and dynamic research frontier [1–12]. Among all 2D materials, graphene, being a gapless semimetal with relativistic energy dispersion, has a special place. The optical and transport properties of such a material can be controlled by considering its bilayer, nanostructures, and quantum dots [13–16]. Investigating optoelectronic properties in finite structures like graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has sparked significant interest, especially in relation to the bandgap tunability based on GQD shape, size, geometry, and edge type [17,18]. Currently, researchers are delving into the generation of high-order harmonics, a product of optical activity, to gain profound insights into the electron dynamics of nanostructured systems interacting with ultrafast optical fields [19–23].

High harmonic generation (HHG) is a highly nonlinear optical process that creates optical pulses with very short wavelengths, from extreme ultraviolet to soft x-rays [24–27]. The high harmonics are generated by intense ultrafast laser pulses interacting with gases or solids [28,29]. In gases, such an energetic optical pulse field overcomes the field that binds electrons to an atom, knocking electrons and accelerating them, and then returning them back to the atom, releasing all of their kinetic energy in the form of high energy photons [30]. In solids, high harmonics are generated due to nonlinear interband and intraband electron dynamics, which is induced by strong optical pulses. The fundamental difference between generation of high harmonics in solids and gasses is that, ionization and recombination of an electron in the field of a pulse occurs at the same atom in gases and at different atoms in solids [31].

During the growth process, defects in solids are inevitably generated [32]. These defects can be of different types, such as vacancies, impurities, dislocations, and others [33–36]. The defects in solids modify their transport and optical properties, which opens a mechanism of controlling the properties of solids by defects engineering [37–40]. Under light-matter interaction, the imperfections such as vacancies or impurities in nanostructures could significantly alter the high harmonic emission spectra, opening up a new avenue for research [41–44]. In Ref. [42], HHG in GQDs with vacancy defects was reported, where the sensitivity of generation of high-order harmonics on the density of the defects were obtained. Such defects break the inversion symmetry for hexagonal GQDs, which results in extra features in the high harmonic spectra [42], where the authors considered only a hexagonal GQD of large size with 216 Carbon atoms placed in a field of a linearly polarized pulse.

In the present paper, we study the generation of high-order harmonics in small GQD of different geometries and different edge types. Namely, we consider triangular and hexagonal GQDs with zigzag and armchair edges placed in the field of a linearly and circularly polarized optical pulse. The triangular and hexagonal GQDs have
D
3 h
and
D
6 h
symmetries, respectively, which determines the properties of the corresponding high-harmonic spectra. We also consider defects in the form of mono- and di-vacancies. Such defects are expected to break the symmetry of the system and modify the radiation spectra.

The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, the model and main equations based on the density matrix approach are introduced. Section 3 discusses the results of calculations and the concluding remarks are summarized in Section 4.

The system of a GQD placed in the field of a short optical pulse is described by the time-dependent Hamiltonian of the following form
(1)
H
( t )
= − t
∑
〈 i j 〉
(
c
ˆ
i
†
c
ˆ
j
+ h . c . )
− e
∑
i
c
ˆ
i
†
c
ˆ
i
r
i
⋅ F
( t )
.
Here, the first term represents the field-free Hamiltonian that describes the GQD system within the scope of the tight-binding model, i and j label the sites of the QD,
c
ˆ
i
†
and
c
ˆ
i
are creation and annihilation operators for an electron at site i , and
t = − 2 . 8
eV
is the hopping integral [45,46]. We also assume that the on-site energies are zero. Numerical diagonalization of the tight binding Hamiltonian for a GQD consisting of N Carbon atoms gives N energy levels with the wave functions
ψ
n
and the corresponding energies
E
n
. Below, the wave functions and the energy spectra are obtained numerically.

The second term describes the interaction of the electron system with the optical field, where
r
i
is the position of the i th atom and
F
( t )
is the time-dependent electric field of an optical pulse. Below we consider both linearly and circularly polarized ultrafast optical pulses for which the time-dependent electric field is defined as
(2)

and, for a circularly polarized pulse, the corresponding components of the optical field are

.

are the amplitude, frequency, and duration of an incident ultrafast optical pulse.

We study the nonlinear electron dynamics of GQD using the time evolution of the density operator
ρ
ˆ
. Such dynamics is described by the following density matrix equation
(5)

. Taking the matrix elements of the left- and right-hand sides of Eq. (5) between the states

(6)

( t )
, and

= e
〈

k

r
ˆ

is the dipole matrix element of the dipole operator
e

ˆ
. The dipole matrix elements are calculated from the following expression
(7)

k n
= e
∑
s

∗
(

)

n
(
r

)
,
where s labels the carbon atoms in a QD and
r
s
is the radius-vector of the s th QD atom.

The density matrix approach allows us to incorporate the relaxation processes into our model through the corresponding phenomenological relaxation rates. To address the relaxation processes corresponding to the dipole decay, we introduce the dephasing time, τ , which we assume is the same for all nondiagonal matrix elements of the density matrix. Below, we set the dephasing time at τ =10 fs. Then the density matrix Eq. (6) takes the following form
(8)
ρ
̇
m n
= i
ω
m n
ρ
m n
+
i
ħ
∑
k
(
ρ
m k
H
k n
′
−
H
m k
′
ρ
k n
)
−
( 1 −
δ
n m
)
ρ
m n
/ τ ,
where
δ
n m
is the Kronecker delta symbol.

Using the interaction representation for the density matrix,
(9)
ρ
̃
m n
=
ρ
m n
e
− i
ω
m n
t
,
we rewrite the system of Eqs. (8) in the following form
(10)
ρ
̃
̇
m n
=
i
ħ
∑
k
ρ
̃
m k
e
i

n k

/ τ .

The system of differential Eqs. (10) is solved numerically using the ODEINT library, which is a collection of different numerical algorithms to solve initial value problems of ordinary differential equations [47]. The initial conditions are that, before the pulse, all the valence band (VB) states are occupied and all the conduction band (CB) states are empty, i.e.,
ρ
̃
n n
= 1
if
n ∈ VB
and
ρ
̃
n n
= 0
if
n ∈ CB
.

With the known solution of the density matrix Eq. (10), the time-dependent dipole moment can be calculated from the following expression
(11)
d
( t )
=
∑
m n
ρ
̃
m n
( t )
e
i
ω
m n
t
D
n m
.
Eq. (11) includes both intraband and interband contributions to the dipole moment.

The time variation of the dipole moment determines the radiation spectrum of the system. At a given frequency ω , the intensity of the corresponding emission is obtained from the following expression
(12)
I
( ω )
=
I
x
( ω )
+
I
y
( ω )
,
where
(13)
I
x
( ω )
=
μ
0
ω
2
12 π c
|
F
ω
[
d
̇
x
]
|
2
,
I
y
( ω )
=
μ
0
ω
2
12 π c
|
F
ω
[
d
̇
y
]
|
2
.
Here,
F
ω
[
d
̇
x
]
|
2
and
F
ω
[
d
̇
y
]
|
2
are frequency Fourier transforms of the time derivatives of the corresponding components of the dipole moment. The order of the generated high harmonic is defined in the units of
ω
0
, i.e.,
(14)
N
ω
=
ω
ω
0
.

The graphene quantum dots considered below are summarized in Table 1. They include both triangular and hexagonal GQDs with zigzag and armchair edges. For each GQD, we also introduced defects in the form of mono- and di-vacancies. The defects changes the energy spectra of the systems, modify the bandgaps, and break the symmetry of the corresponding GQDs and electron states. Without defects, a triangular GQD has
D
3 h
symmetry, while a hexagonal GQD has
D
6 h
symmetry. The GQDs with zigzag edges also have in-gap edge states. The bandgaps for intrinsic QDs and QDs with defects are also presented in Table 1. The results clearly show that the presence of the defects suppresses the bandgap, which is due to the fact that the defects produces in-gap states that changes the corresponding bandgaps.

The energy spectra of GQDs with their corresponding structures are shown in Figs. 1, 2, and 3. The hexagonal QDs, studied below, are shown in Fig. 1. We consider three different sizes of QDs with
N = 24
, 54, and 84 atoms for intrinsic systems. In this way, we can cover small, medium, and large GQDs. For each QD, we introduce mono- and di-vacancies as shown in Fig. 1. They are introduced near the corresponding QD centers. The intrinsic hexagonal QD has
D
3 h
symmetry. Some elements of such a symmetry group are broken by introducing the vacancies. The vacancies also produces in-gap electron states, which are clearly visible in Fig. 1 and results in suppression of the bandgap of the system.

For triangular QD, we consider two types of QDs: with zigzag and armchair edges. Both types of triangular QDs have
D
3 h
symmetry. The fundamental difference between these two types of edges is that for triangular GQD with zigzag edges there are degenerate in-gap edge states with zero energies, while there are no such states in triangular GQDs with armchair edges.

The energy spectra of triangular QDs with zigzag edges are shown in Fig. 2 for different sizes of GQDs. The spectra are characterized by the presence of in-gap edge states with zero energy. The number of such in-gap states changes with the size of the system and the number of introduced vacancies.

The energy spectra of triangular QDs with armchair edges are shown in Fig. 3 for two sizes of the QD with 36 and 60 atoms. In this case, for the intrinsic QD, there is a clear gap between the valence and conduction states, while the vacancies introduce additional in-gap states, which suppresses the corresponding band gap.

The emission spectra of hexagonal QDs are shown in Fig. 4 for different sizes of QDs and different numbers of vacancies,
N
v a c
. The results are shown for linearly, see Fig. 4(a), (c), and (e), and for circularly, see Fig. 4(b), (d), and (f), polarized pulses. For a linearly polarized pulse, see Fig. 4(a), (c), and (e), due to inversion symmetry of a hexagonal QD, the even order high-harmonics are suppressed. When one vacancy is added to a QD, which breaks the inversion symmetry of the system, the even harmonics become strong. Such an enhancement of even-order harmonics is more pronounced for a small QD, see Fig. 4(a), while for a larger QD,
N = 54
, see Fig. 4(c), intensities of even-order harmonics increase but they are still relatively weak. For even larger QD,
N = 84
, see Fig. 4(e), the intensities of even-order harmonics are almost the same as for the intrinsic QD. The enhancement of high-order harmonics, around order 18, by monovacancy is clearly visible for an intermediate QD, see Fig. 4(c). When the second vacancy is added to the system, the even-order harmonics become suppressed again. This is because, for the arrangement of two vacancies considered in the present paper, the hexagonal QDs with two vacancies have inversion symmetry.

For a circularly polarized pulse, for intrinsic hexagonal QDs, not only even harmonics but also harmonics, which are multiples of 3, i.e.,
3 m
, m is an integer, orders 3, 6, 9, and so on, are also suppressed, see Fig. 4(b), (d), and (f). This is due to
D
6 h
symmetry of the system. In this case, when one or two vacancies are added to QDs, which breaks
D
6 h
symmetry, the
3 m
harmonics are strongly enhanced. Interesting, that even for a large QD with 84 atoms, see Fig. 4(f), the enhancement of
3 m
harmonics is very strong, where the intensities of high harmonics are almost the same for the cases of mono- and di-vacancies. Similar behavior is visible for an intermediate QD of the size of
N = 54
, see Fig. 4(d). At the same time, for a small QD, see Fig. 4(b), the intensities of high-order harmonics are the strongest for the system with one vacancy. For QDs with di-vacancy, the QDs have an inversion symmetry, which results in suppression of even-order harmonics for such systems.

The radiation spectra of triangular QDs with zigzag edges are shown in Fig. 5. For a linearly polarized light, even for intrinsic QDs, all harmonics are allowed and visible, where the odd harmonics are generated by the x component of the dipole moment, while the even harmonics are generated by the y component of the dipole moment. Here, the pulse is polarized along the x -axis. The reason for such a selection is following. For an even harmonic
2 n
, the x and y components of the generated dipole moment can be expressed as
d
x
=
α
x
F
x
2 n
and
d
y
=
α
y
F
x
2 n
. Since the x -axis is the axis of symmetry, then under a reflection with respect to the x -axis, the relations between
d
x
,
d
y
and
E
x
remain valid, and since
F
x
→ −
F
x
and
F
x
2 n
→
F
x
2 n
,
d
x
→ −
d
x
, and
d
y
→
d
y
, then
α
x
= 0
and
α
y
≠ 0
, which means the even harmonics are generated by the y -component of the dipole moment only. When the vacancies are introduced, the intensities of high-order harmonics are increased. While for intermediate and large QDs with sizes of 33 and 61 atoms, the changes in the intensities are not strong, for small QD with the size of
N = 22
the high harmonics are strongly enhanced when the vacancies are present, see Fig. 5(a). Here, for the second order harmonic, the largest intensity is realized for the system with a mono-vacancy, while for the higher order harmonics,
> 3
, the system with di-vacancies produces the strongest harmonics.

The responses of triangular QDs to a circularly polarized pulse, see Fig. 5(b), (d), and (f), are similar to the ones of hexagonal QDs. Namely, for intrinsic triangular QDs, harmonics of order
3 m
, where m is an integer, are suppressed due to
D
3 h
symmetry of the system. Namely, for
3 m
-order harmonics,
d =
α
3 m
F
3 m
. Because of
D
3 h
symmetry of the system, a rotation by the angle of
12
0
0
should not change the above relation, but such a rotation does not change
F
3 m
, while changes d . Therefore
α
3 m
should be zero and
3 m
-order high harmonics are suppressed. The presence of vacancies breaks the symmetry resulting in strong
3 m
-order harmonics. Such strong enhancement is visible for all sizes of QDs, where the intensities of high harmonics for the systems with mono- and di-vacancies are comparable. The fact that, for all sizes of QDs, the
3 m
harmonics are strongly enhanced when any type of defect is present illustrates that such harmonics are strongly sensitive to the properties of electron states near the center of QDs, i.e., near the positions of vacancies. The generation of high harmonics is mainly determined by interaction of in-gap edge states and the bulk states near the edges of the valence and conduction band. While the in-gap edge states are mainly located near the edges of QDs, the bulk states are mainly localized near the centers of QDs, which results in sensitivity of radiation spectra to the presence of defects.

The main difference between triangular QDs with zigzag and armchair edges is the existence of in-gap edge states for systems with zigzag edges, while for QDs with armchair edges there are no such states. This property affects the radiation spectra of the systems. In Fig. 6, the radiation spectra of triangular QDs with armchair edges are shown. The main difference from the corresponding spectra of the QDs with zigzag edges is the suppression of generation of even harmonics for intrinsic QDs, see Fig. 6. Such suppression is due to the fact that the main contribution to generation of high harmonics comes from the interband dynamics, which couples the electron states near the bottom of the conduction band and the top of the valence band. The corresponding wave functions are localized near the center of a QD and have the symmetry that is close to
D
6 h
, for which the even harmonics are suppressed. For triangular QDs with zigzag edges, the high harmonics are generated due to electron dynamics between the in-gap edge states, which are localized near the edges of QDs, and the bulk states near the top of the valence band and the bottom of the conduction band. The in-gap edge states have well pronounced
D
3 h
symmetry, which allows generation of even harmonics.

When the vacancies are added to triangular QDs with armchair edges, see Figs. 6 (a) and (c), even-order harmonics are strongly enhanced. For both small,
N = 36
, and large,
N = 60
, QDs, the QDs with mono-vacancy generates higher intensity even-order harmonics than the QDs with di-vacancy. This is because when the second vacancy is added to the system, as shown in Fig. 3, the local inversion symmetry near the center of a QD is restored, which results in partial suppression of even-order harmonics in the emission spectra. Such behavior means that the main contribution to the generation of even-order harmonics in a triangular QD with armchair edges comes from the bulk states, which are localized near the center of the QD.

For a circularly polarized pulse, see Fig. 6(b) and (d), there is a strong suppression of both even and
3 m
harmonics, which is similar to the case of hexagonal QDs. The suppression of even harmonics is due to local inversion symmetry near the center of triangular QDs, while the suppression of
3 m
harmonics is due to
D
3 h
symmetry of triangular QDs. When one vacancy is added to a QD, it breaks both symmetries resulting in strong enhancement of high harmonic generation, which is clearly visible for both small and large QDs. When the second vacancy is added to a QD, it restores local inversion symmetry but
D
3 h
symmetry remains broken. As a result, even order harmonics become partially suppressed for QDs with di-vacancies, while
3 m
harmonics remain strong and comparable to the case of QDs with mono-vacancies.

The above results show that the radiation spectra of small QDs are the most sensitive to the presence of the defects. In Fig. 7, we illustrate how this sensitivity depends on the amplitude of the optical pulse. For a linearly polarized incident pulse, the spectra are shown for the triangular QD with zigzag edges and the size of
N = 22
, see Fig. 7(a) and the hexagonal QD of the size
N = 24
, see Fig. 7(b). The general tendency in the behavior of radiation spectra remains the same for all field amplitudes. Namely, for the triangular QD, see Fig. 7(a), the generation of high-order harmonics can be enhanced by adding vacancies to the systems, where the second-order harmonic has the strongest intensity for the QD with a mono-vacancy, while the higher-order harmonics,
> 3
, are the strongest when the system has a di-vacancy. For the hexagonal QD, Fig. 7(b), the enhancement of high harmonics is the strongest for the QD with mono-vacancy. Also, the mono-vacancy breaks the inversion symmetry of the system, which results in generation of strong even-order harmonics.

The radiation spectra generated in a circularly polarized pulse are shown in Fig. 8 for different field amplitudes for a triangular QD, see Fig. 8(a), and a hexagonal QD, see 8(b). With increasing the field amplitude, more harmonics are generated, which is expected in general case. Also, with increasing
F
0
, the higher-order harmonics become more sensitive to the presence of the defects and their number. For example, for a hexagonal QD, for the field amplitude of
F
0
= 0 . 1
V/Å, and for the highest generated harmonics around orders 6–10, the intensities of such harmonics are almost the same for an intrinsic QD and QDs with defects, see Fig. 8(b). At the same time, for the field amplitude of
F
0
= 0 . 5
V/Å and harmonic orders around 12–18, the intensities of high-order harmonics strongly depend on the presence of the defect with the largest intensities generated for the system with a mono-vacancy. Also, for the systems with vacancies, the cut-off frequency, i.e., the maximum generated order, is strongly enhanced by the defects. Namely, for the field amplitude of
F
0
= 0 . 5
V/Å, the cut-off frequency for the intrinsic hexagonal QD is around 13, while the corresponding cut-off frequency is 18 for the QD with a mono-vacancy and 17 for the QD with a di-vacancy. The reason for such an increase in the cut-off frequency is that, although with the presence of vacancies, the number of atoms in a QD decreases, the vacancies introduce extra nonlinearity into ultrafast electron dynamics. This is because the vacancies introduce local irregularity in the arrangement of atoms in a QD.

Generation of high-order harmonics in zero dimensional systems can be controlled by introducing defects into the systems. One type of the defects is a vacancy, which can be in the form of a monovacancy or a divacancy. For graphene QDs, the effect of the defects on radiation spectra depends on the geometry of a QD, its symmetry, and the type of the edges. In the present paper, we considered three types of graphene QDs: hexagonal QDs with zigzag edges, triangular QDs with zigzag edges, and triangular QDs with armchair edges. For an intrinsic hexagonal QD, the system has an inversion symmetry, which results in strong suppression of even-order harmonics. Introducing one vacancy into the system breaks the inversion symmetry and generates strong even harmonics for both linearly and circularly polarized incident pulses. The second vacancy can be added into the system in a such way that it can restore the system’s inversion symmetry, which results in suppression of even-order harmonics similar to the intrinsic case. For a circularly polarized pulse, another effect can be observed. Namely, due to
D
6 h
symmetry of a hexagonal QD, the harmonics of orders
3 m
, where m is an integer, are suppressed in the field of a circularly polarized pulse. In this case, both mono- and di-vacancies break
D
6 h
symmetry, resulting in strong enhancement of
3 m
-order harmonics. Similar breaking of QD
D
3 h
symmetry for triangular QDs by mono- and di-vacancies results in the enhancement of
3 m
-order high harmonics in triangular QDs placed in the field of a circularly polarized pulse. Such an effect is visible for triangular QDs with both zigzag and armchair edges.

For triangular QDs with armchair edges, there is an another symmetry-related effect. Namely, such QDs have local inversion symmetry visible in the bulk of QDs. Such local symmetry suppresses generation of even-order harmonics in intrinsic QDs and similar to hexagonal QDs, when one vacancy is added to the system, the local inversion symmetry becomes broken and the generation of even harmonics is strongly enhanced. Also, when the second vacancy is added to the QD, which restores the local inversion symmetry, then even-order harmonics become suppressed again.

Thus, the defects in graphene QDs can break the symmetry of the system, which can strongly enhance the generation of some high harmonics that are symmetry-forbidden in the intrinsic systems. Such an effect is visible even if a single vacancy is added to the graphene system.
Suresh Gnawali: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Vadym Apalkov: Writing – review & editing, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Major funding was provided by Grant No. DE-FG02-01ER15213 from the Chemical Sciences, Biosciences and Geosciences Division, Office of Basic Energy Sciences, Office of Science, US Department of Energy. Numerical simulations were performed using support by Grant No. DE-SC0007043 from the Materials Sciences and Engineering Division of the Office of the Basic Energy Sciences, Office of Science, US Department of Energy .
